titleStmt.title: Memorandum of the Substance of Discussion at a Department of State Joint Chiefs of Staff Meeting

head.hi: Memorandum of the Substance of Discussion at a Department of State Joint Chiefs of Staff Meeting

note.p: A note on the title page read: “State Draft. Not cleared with any of the participants.” Generals Bradley, Vandenberg, and Collins and Admiral Fechteler attended for the Joint Chiefs of Staff. Matthews headed the Department of State participants. Nash attended for the Department of Defense, and Lay represented the National Security Council. In all, 19 persons attended.
Korea did not form a substantial part of the discussion. The other topics dealt with were Japan, Southeast Asia, and Trieste.

div.p: General Bradley asked whether in the view of the State Department it would be helpful or hurtful for the Air Force to strike at power plants in North Korea—not the plants on the Yalu but plants as close as ten miles to the Yalu. Some of these plants supply power to the Soviet Union. The Air Force has not been hitting these plants for some time.

Mr. Matthews stated that the Department of State had never objected to the bombing of power plants. Mr. Bohlen thought that it might be actually helpful as a means of expediting the negotiations. He emphasized the importance of not straying over the frontiers, particularly in Northeast Korea.

There was some discussion of the question whether Russians are flying MIG’s in North Korea. General Vandenberg said that no Russian pilots had been captured but that the pilots were speaking Russian on their intercoms.

Mr. Matthews said that he had noticed with some concern that the Soviet Union was building up a good North Korean air force. It was his impression that we were not doing this in South Korea. He thought this might have some serious implications for the future.

General Vandenberg said that it was a long process to build up an air force and that the Russians had probably been working at it for several years. Mr. Bohlen said that he had noticed a pattern of some interest. Apparently the Soviet Union has concentrated its energy and resources on the training and supplying of North Koreans rather than Chinese Communists. He thought that this allocation of effort was continuing and found some confirmation of this in the fact that we were beginning to see North Korean air divisions. General Vandenberg thought that if we knew the facts we would probably find that Chinese Communists, Russians and Germans were all included in the North Korean air force. He thought that it was impossible to get as many good pilots as were obviously available from North Korea alone. Mr. Bohlen pointed out that there were many Soviet Koreans who had served in the Soviet Army during World War II. Some of these might be employed.
